status: HELD — merged by grow.mjs then withdrawn before publication.
semantics: Cards whose performer and role are verified but whose year of performance could not be sourced. Memory Alpha episode pages carry in-universe stardates in their infobox and bury the real broadcast date in unstructured production timelines, so the year was not machine-resolvable. The roster schema requires years, and inventing one to satisfy it would be exactly the fuzzy-fact failure the Wikipedia gate cannot catch. Re-merge each once its air year is sourced.

rows.knownFor: Suzie Plakson is a prolific genre television actress recognized for playing three different alien and human characters across Star Trek: The Next Generation.
rows.reveal: As the Vulcan physician Selar, Plakson performs beneath upswept prosthetic ears and a Vulcan brow. It is one of three distinct roles she took on across The Next Generation, alongside the Klingon K'Ehleyr and a female Q.

rows.knownFor: Nana Visitor is best known for playing Bajoran first officer Kira Nerys throughout Star Trek: Deep Space Nine.
rows.reveal: Nana Visitor took on several one-off Bajoran guest roles on Deep Space Nine beyond her regular part as Kira, wearing the same style of nose-ridge prosthetic the production applied to Bajoran characters throughout the series. Off-screen she went on to direct episodes of the franchise as well as act in it.

rows.knownFor: Nana Visitor is best known for playing Bajoran first officer Kira Nerys throughout Star Trek: Deep Space Nine.
rows.reveal: Beyond her regular part as Kira Nerys, Nana Visitor occasionally took on additional Bajoran guest roles on Deep Space Nine, applied with the same style of nose-ridge prosthetic used for the species throughout the series. She continued working in television as both an actress and director after the show ended.

rows.knownFor: Kaitlin Hopkins is a stage and screen actress who originated roles in Off-Broadway musical theatre before later heading the musical theatre program at Texas State University.
rows.reveal: Under Kilana's pale, ridged Vorta prosthetics and opaque black contact lenses is Kaitlin Hopkins, an American actress trained in musical theatre. She went on to build a second career as a musical-theatre educator, work entirely apart from the alien diplomat she played on Deep Space Nine.

rows.knownFor: Larry Cedar is a veteran American character actor with decades of television guest-starring credits.
rows.reveal: Larry Cedar played the Kazon leader Tersa in Star Trek: Voyager, one of three separate alien roles credited to him across the franchise. The Kazon's heavy ridged forehead and matted wig left little of Cedar's own face visible on screen.

rows.knownFor: Larry Cedar is a veteran American character actor with decades of television guest-starring credits.
rows.reveal: Larry Cedar played the T'Lani official Nydrom in Star Trek: Deep Space Nine, one of three separate alien roles credited to him in the franchise. As with his other Star Trek appearances, alien prosthetic makeup obscured his features enough that the role is rarely traced back to him.

rows.knownFor: Clint Howard is a prolific character actor and younger brother of director Ron Howard, with a career spanning Apollo 13, Austin Powers, and decades of television guest roles.
rows.reveal: Two decades after voicing Balok on the original series, Howard returned to Star Trek buried under a Ferengi's ridged ears and cranial prosthetics, unrecognizable beneath the design. It is a frequently cited piece of trivia among fans tracking his sprawling, decades-long list of credits.

rows.knownFor: Michael G. Hagerty is a veteran character actor best known for his recurring role as Larry, the building superintendent, on the sitcom Friends.
rows.reveal: Hagerty's face disappeared under alien prosthetic makeup for this one-off Voyager guest role, the kind of transformation easy to miss on a rewatch. Off the ridges, he spent the 1990s as a familiar sitcom presence, most durably as the plumber Chandler and Joey kept calling back.

rows.knownFor: Mary Elizabeth McGlynn is a prolific voice actress and ADR director best known for voicing Major Motoko Kusanagi in the English dub of Ghost in the Shell: Stand Alone Complex.
rows.reveal: Long before fans knew her voice from anime dubs and video games, McGlynn took a rare on-camera role on Voyager, hidden under alien prosthetic makeup rather than showing her own face. She has spent most of her career behind the microphone, directing and performing English-language dubbing for major anime and game franchises.

rows.knownFor: Mary Elizabeth McGlynn is a prolific voice actress and ADR director best known for voicing Major Motoko Kusanagi in the English dub of Ghost in the Shell: Stand Alone Complex.
rows.reveal: This was one of two brief Voyager guest appearances for McGlynn, both performed under alien facial prosthetics rather than her own face. She's far better known today as a voice actress and dubbing director, having lent her voice and direction to major anime and video game franchises.

rows.knownFor: Aron Eisenberg is best known for playing the Ferengi Nog across the run of Star Trek: Deep Space Nine.
rows.reveal: Beyond his signature role as Nog, Aron Eisenberg took on other alien guest parts under Star Trek's prosthetic makeup teams during the 1990s. He lived with kidney disease from childhood and underwent multiple transplants over his lifetime.

rows.knownFor: John Schuck is a character actor best known for playing Painless Pole in the film M*A*S*H and Sgt. Charles Enright on the TV series McMillan & Wife.
rows.reveal: John Schuck is a journeyman American character actor whose career runs from 1970s Robert Altman films through decades of episodic television. As the Cardassian officer Parn on Deep Space Nine, his face is fully reworked with the ridged Cardassian forehead, neck, and ear prosthetics used throughout the series.

Edward Wiley played the Cardassian Gul Toran on Deep Space Nine, one of two heavily prostheticized alien roles he took in the franchise. The Cardassian makeup covers his forehead, neck, and ears, leaving little of his own face on screen.

rows.knownFor: Andrew Prine is a prolific genre actor known for the 1983 miniseries 'V' and the horror film 'Grizzly.'
rows.reveal: Andrew Prine, a genre-television actor working steadily since the 1960s, played the Cardassian Gul Turrel on Deep Space Nine. The ridged Cardassian prosthetics cover his forehead, neck, and ears, concealing his familiar face.

Wiley also appeared as the Klingon governor Vagh in the Next Generation episode 'The Mind's Eye,' wearing the ridged Klingon forehead prosthetic used throughout the franchise. Both of his Star Trek roles required him to perform entirely through applied prosthetic makeup rather than his own face.

Darryl Henriques played the Romulan senator Nanclus under the pointed ears, ridged brow, and grey-toned skin standard to Romulan appliance makeup of the era. Strip the prosthetics away and it's an American character actor whose own features never register on screen.

Caitlin Brown played Ty Kajada, a Kobliad security officer, under the ridged temple and brow appliance that marked the species on screen. The actress's own face is fully intact underneath it — a single-zone prosthetic, not a full transformation.

Daniel Riordan disappears under a full set of Klingon forehead ridges and braided hair to play one of the elders aboard a generations-old Klingon colony ship. It's one of two entirely different alien roles he's credited with in the franchise, each built from a fresh prosthetic rather than a returning character.

references.label: Duras, son of Toral at Memory Alpha

rows.reveal: As the Zaldan examiner Rondon, Riordan's face is reworked with an alien brow and skin appliance for a single first-season TNG episode. He returns to Star Trek more than a decade later under a completely different Klingon prosthetic, credited each time as a different species entirely.

Henry Woronicz plays the Klingon scientist J'Dan under a standard forehead-ridge appliance, one of two unrelated alien roles he's credited with across the franchise. Nothing of his own brow or hairline reads through the prosthetic.

Five years after playing a ridge-browed Klingon on TNG, Woronicz returns under a completely different alien design as the Kyrian Quarren. Same performer, same era of Westmore-shop prosthetics, two unrelated species with nothing visually in common.

In the same episode that closes out Kurn's story, Todd plays Rodek — the false identity implanted in Kurn's mind-wiped body — under the identical Klingon prosthetic design. The makeup never changes; only the performance underneath shifts to a character who doesn't know his own history.

Ivar Brogger plays the alien administrator Barus under a full facial prosthetic redesign, one of two unrelated alien roles he's credited with in the franchise. His own features don't surface anywhere in the performance.

Nearly a decade before his Voyager appearance, Brogger plays the Romulan technician Orum under pointed ears and an alien brow. Two separate credits, two separate prosthetic builds, neither one showing the performer as himself.
